one_line_takeaway: Restricting a deployed ECG foundation encoder to scalar scores does not guarantee participation privacy: objective-aligned scores and, especially, exported embeddings (near-perfect k-NN detection for contrastive encoders on small cohorts) can both leak whether a subject was in the pretraining set.
key_finding: Leakage is strongly regime-dependent rather than a uniform property of SSL objectives. Smallest/most homogeneous cohort BUTQDB shows the most severe leakage: learned-attack SimCLR-CNN reaches [AUC 0.931 | TPR@0.01 0.833 | adv 0.823]; score-only MAE-CNN reaches [0.944 | 0.667 | 0.657], the largest score-only AUC observed. Larger/more diverse cohorts attenuate leakage (e.g., MITDB learned SimCLR-CNN [0.585|0.301|0.291]; SHAREE score-only MAE-CNN collapses to [0.490|0.000|0.000]). Learned attackers are not uniformly stronger than score-only thresholding: on BUTQDB, MAE-CNN AUC drops from 0.944 (score-only) to 0.722 (learned), Delta AUC = -0.222, showing a concentrated objective-aligned scalar can already be the worst-case interface. Embedding-access attacks reveal the most severe channel for contrastive encoders: on BUTQDB and CHFDB, both SimCLR-CNN and TS2Vec achieve [1.000|1.000|0.990] via a simple k-NN probe on mean-pooled embeddings -- perfect subject-level detection at fpr=0.01. MAE embeddings, by contrast, often leak less than their own reconstruction-error scalars (e.g., BUTQDB MAE-CNN: embedding [0.750|0.571|0.561] vs score-only [0.944|0.667|0.657]). TPR@0.01 frequently diverges sharply from AUC (e.g., TS2Vec on LTDB: [0.522|0.000|0.000] learned), showing bulk separability does not imply tail (high-confidence) attack success, and vice versa.
core_contribution: An implementation-grounded audit of membership inference attacks (MIAs) against self-supervised ECG foundation encoders. The authors pretrain four SSL encoder families (SimCLR-CNN, TS2Vec, MAE-CNN, MAE-Transformer) on five PhysioNet ECG datasets (BUTQDB, CHFDB, LTDB, MITDB, SHAREE) and evaluate three attacker interfaces aligned with realistic connected-health deployments: (i) score-only black-box access (reconstruction error for MAE, augmentation-consistency cosine similarity for contrastive encoders), (ii) an adaptive learned attacker (a 2-layer MLP, 4->16->1, trained on subject-level summary statistics -- mean, std, max, 90th percentile -- of window scores), and (iii) an embedding-access attacker using k-NN proximity (k=5) over mean-pooled subject-level embeddings. They introduce a subject-centric auditing protocol with window-to-subject top-k aggregation (k=50 of up to w=2000 windows/subject), calibration at a fixed false-positive rate (fpr=0.01), and a cross-dataset non-member construction (non-members sampled from auxiliary PhysioNet datasets disjoint from the training dataset) to model realistic institution/cohort-level participation leakage. This is the paper's own empirical contribution, not a survey of prior MIA work.
threat_model: Connected-health deployment: a foundation ECG encoder is pretrained on a private patient cohort (S_train) and reused across tasks/institutions via model-as-a-service interfaces. An adversary observes the deployed encoder's outputs (never raw waveforms or labels) and tries to infer the participation indicator y(s*) = 1[s* in S_train] for a target subject. Three observable-interface classes are formalized: score-only (scalar reconstruction error / anomaly / similarity score), aggregated-statistic (repeated queries aggregated into subject-level features), and embedding-access (exported latent feature vectors for retrieval/clustering/downstream training, i.e. a 'feature API'). Ground-truth membership labels come from the pretraining pipeline's recorded train_ids.json, not oracle attacker access. The paper explicitly notes representation-access attackers beyond k-NN probing, adaptive query-optimization strategies, and transfer attacks through fine-tuned downstream models are left to future work.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) The cross-dataset non-member construction may conflate true subject-level membership signal with dataset/institution-specific confounds (acquisition hardware, voltage scaling, sensor noise, resampling artifacts) -- high attack performance does not imply pure subject identification, only participation/institution-level leakage. (2) Uniform resampling to 250 Hz across heterogeneous original sampling rates may introduce consistent, dataset-detectable interpolation artifacts that could inflate cross-dataset separability. (3) Evaluation covers only score-only, learned subject-level, and simple k-NN embedding-access attackers; broader attack surfaces -- more sophisticated representation-access attacks, adaptive query-optimization, transfer attacks via fine-tuned downstream models, and longitudinal/temporal membership inference under model updates -- are explicitly left to future work. (4) No defenses (e.g., differential privacy, calibrated noise) are implemented or evaluated; the paper only discusses these as future directions. (5) Single-dataset pretraining regime only; multi-site harmonized pretraining is not studied.

datasets_used: BUTQDB (Brno University of Technology ECG Quality Database, PhysioNet) -- 344,660 used windows
CHFDB (congestive heart failure long-term ECG, PhysioNet) -- 215,177 used windows
LTDB (Long-Term ST database, PhysioNet) -- 106,157 used windows
MITDB (MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database, PhysioNet) -- 20,017 used windows
SHAREE (ambulatory hypertensive-cohort ECG, PhysioNet) -- 2,296,925 used windows
quantitative_results: Learned attack, BUTQDB, SimCLR-CNN: AUC 0.931 | TPR@0.01 0.833 | adv 0.823
Score-only attack, BUTQDB, MAE-CNN: AUC 0.944 | TPR@0.01 0.667 | adv 0.657 (largest score-only AUC observed)
Score-only attack, CHFDB, MAE-Transformer: AUC 0.720 | TPR@0.01 0.625 | adv 0.615
Embedding-access attack (kNN, k=5), BUTQDB & CHFDB, SimCLR-CNN and TS2Vec: AUC 1.000 | TPR@0.01 1.000 | adv 0.990 (perfect detection)
Embedding-access attack, BUTQDB, MAE-CNN: AUC 0.750 | TPR@0.01 0.571 | adv 0.561 (lower than its own score-only leakage)
Learned attack, MITDB, SimCLR-CNN: AUC 0.585 | TPR@0.01 0.301 | adv 0.291 (moderate/diffuse regime)
Score-only attack, SHAREE, MAE-CNN: AUC 0.490 | TPR@0.01 0.000 | adv 0.000 (attenuated in large diverse cohort)
Delta AUC (learned - score-only), BUTQDB MAE-CNN: -0.222 (learning underperforms a concentrated scalar signal)
Delta AUC, SHAREE MAE-CNN: score-only 0.490 -> learned 0.667 (learning helps when signal is diffuse)
baselines_compared: MAE-Transformer (masked reconstruction, Transformer with patch embedding)
Compared across three attacker strengths: score-only scalar rule, learned MLP subject-level attacker, and kNN embedding-space attacker
